preFix.title: Honey and almond cake
preFix.subtitle: A moist, golden cake sweetened entirely with honey
preFix.excerpt: Ground almonds make up more than half the dry weight in this cake, giving a tender, slightly fudgy crumb that stays moist for three days. The sweetness comes entirely from honey, which also adds a floral depth and a slight chew to the crust. The cake needs to be watched during baking as honey accelerates browning. Preparation is about 20 minutes.
preFix.sourceNotes: Honey-sweetened almond cakes have a long history across the Mediterranean and Middle East, appearing in Spanish mazapan, Greek amygdalopita and Ottoman-influenced pastries. The British tradition of honey cakes appears in Hannah Glasse's 'The Art of Cookery Made Plain and Easy' (1747). The combination of ground almonds and honey as the primary sweetener is documented in Claudia Roden's 'The Book of Jewish Food' (1997) in the context of Passover baking.

content.text: This cake uses no refined sugar at all:

produces a different result from caster sugar: denser, moister, and with a crust that browns quickly. Use a mild honey such as acacia or clover rather than a strong heather or buckwheat honey, which can overpower the almond.

The ground almonds make this cake naturally gluten-light: there is more almond than flour in the dry mix. The texture is closer to a frangipane than a sponge, with a moist, slightly fudgy crumb. Do not overbake: the centre should still feel slightly soft when the skewer comes out clean.

items.name: Honey
items.prepNote: mild, such as acacia or clover

content.text: Heat the oven to 165 degrees C fan. Grease and line a 20 cm round cake tin. Beat the 125 g softened butter until creamy, then beat in the 150 g honey until combined. The mixture will look slightly curdled at this stage; this is normal. Beat in the 3 eggs one at a time, then the 1 tsp vanilla extract. Fold in the 150 g ground almonds, 100 g flour and 1 tsp baking powder until just combined.

Pour into the prepared tin and scatter with 30 g flaked almonds. Bake for 40 to 50 minutes, checking from 25 minutes in. If the top is colouring quickly, lay a sheet of foil loosely over the tin. The cake is done when the top is deep golden brown, the flaked almonds are toasted and dark at the edges, and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean or with just a few moist crumbs. Cool in the tin for 20 minutes, then on a rack.

items.problem: Top is very dark but centre is underbaked
items.solution: Honey browns faster than sugar. Cover with foil from the 25-minute mark and reduce the temperature to 160 degrees C. The extra baking time needed is the same regardless of the foil covering.

items.problem: Batter split when the eggs were added
items.solution: Honey and butter do not cream together as smoothly as butter and sugar. A slightly split-looking batter at this stage will come back together once the flour and almonds are folded in. If it looks very separated, beat in 1 tbsp of the measured flour before adding the remaining eggs.

items.problem: Cake is very dense and barely rose
items.solution: The baking powder may have been left out or expired. Check the baking powder is fresh: it should bubble immediately when a pinch is dropped into hot water.

initialFindings.message: paragraph reads at grade 11.5 (qc threshold: 11) — simplify (target grade 6-8)
